_meta.purpose: Research reference for drafting ORIGINAL plan content on this site. Not for direct publishing of source text.
_meta.extraction_method: Manual browse via Claude in Chrome, freewoodworkingplan.com directory -> outbound source site
_meta.license_caveat: Source sites hold standard copyright on their own written instructions/photos (no CC/reuse license observed on any source checked so far). For each plan: 'materials'/'cut_list'/'tools'/'dimensions'/'tags' are factual data pulled from the source (not copyrightable expression). 'original_description' and 'original_steps' are written fresh, in different words and structure than any source, derived only from those facts. Do not copy source instructional prose, narrative, or photos into site content.
_meta.site_categories: This site's actual 6 categories (from content/categories.json): Cutting Boards, Furniture, Storage & Shelving, Outdoor, Shop Projects, Gifts & Small Projects. From this point on, extraction is prioritized against these 6 rather than sweeping all 195 directory categories alphabetically.
_meta.directory_scope: The full directory has 195 categories (see wp-sitemap-taxonomies-category-1.xml) and likely thousands of individual plan posts across 5 post sitemaps - a full alphabetical sweep is not practical in one session and was abandoned in favor of matching this site's 6 categories.
_meta.tooling_note: Switched extraction method mid-session: instead of read_page (which truncates category listings to ~2 items and sometimes returns a 0x0 viewport / empty result), use javascript_tool to query the DOM directly - e.g. `Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('article a')).map(a=>({text:a.textContent.trim(),href:a.href}))` on a category page returns the FULL list (usually 10 items, not 2), and `.find(h=>!h.includes('freewoodworkingplan.com'))` on a teaser page reliably grabs the outbound source link in one call. Much higher hit rate and fewer round trips than the read_page approach used earlier in this file. THEN, for real scale, this was replaced entirely by a bulk Python (requests + BeautifulSoup + ThreadPoolExecutor) scrape run in /tmp via the bash sandbox: one script resolved category-page -> post -> outbound-source-link for 26 categories x 4 posts (108 links, 0% dead-link rate) in under 20 seconds, and a second script fetched full page text for 24 chosen outbound URLs in parallel in under 15 seconds. This is dramatically faster than one-page-per-browser-turn and is the method to use for any further bulk extraction. Scripts and raw dumps live in /tmp/scrape/ (sandbox-local, not the mounted repo) and were not persisted to the repo since they're throwaway tooling, not content.
_meta.categories_remaining: 78 plans extracted across all 6 site categories, most with 3+ entries each (Cutting Boards: 4, Furniture: 8, Storage & Shelving: 8, Outdoor: 8, Shop Projects: 9, Gifts & Small Projects: 11 - remainder are the earlier general finds). Two full passes through the bulk-scraper index (27 categories x 4 sampled posts = 108 links) are now done. The 'carvings' category (4/4 posts) is 100% instructables.com and was skipped entirely - no usable source found. A few remaining posts per category are still untouched (3rd/4th sample) if more depth is ever wanted, but coverage is now broad and even across all 6 site categories - this is a reasonable stopping point for the research phase.

plans.title: 6-Board Leopold Bench
plans.dimensions: Not given as text on source page (diagram/image only)
cut_list: 2 - 2x8 @ 35" - both ends cut at 30 degrees off square, ends parallel
2 - 2x8 @ 18" - both ends cut at 30 degrees off square, ends parallel
plans.original_description: A classic 6-board outdoor bench with a slanted seat and backrest, built entirely from three dimensional lumber boards. No jigs or special joinery, just angled crosscuts and screws, making it one of the fastest beginner outdoor seating builds.
original_steps: Crosscut all pieces per the cut list; the two 35" and two 18" leg pieces get 30-degree angled ends (a miter saw is easiest for accuracy).
On the two longer (35") legs, trim the back edge with a matching 30-degree cut so the leg sits flush against the shorter leg once assembled.
Stand a shorter (18") leg against a longer (35") leg, align the top edges, and fasten with 2-3/4" screws to form each leg assembly; repeat for both sides.
Attach the 2x6 backrest across the tops of the two leg assemblies, keeping the top edges flush.
Set the frame on a flat surface and fasten the 2x10 seat board across the tops of the legs, centered, screwing from both the top and the outside face of the long legs.
Sand all edges and finish with an exterior stain or paint.

plans.title: Barn Bird House and Feeder
plans.dimensions: Overall not stated; built from cedar fence boards, one birdhouse in front, one on side, feeder on opposite side

cut_list.size: 5-1/2in x 11-1/2in, 45-degree angle; one with 1-1/2in entrance hole and 3/8in dowel hole

plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download of this plan
plans.original_description: A combination birdhouse-and-feeder built from cedar fence boards, with one nesting box facing front, a second nesting box on one side, and an open feeder tray on the opposite side, under a shared peaked roof.
original_steps: Cut all lettered parts (A-J) to the sizes and bevel angles in the cut list.
Glue and nail the base panel (A) to the angled back panel (B) to form the main body shell.
Attach the four small angled corner pieces (C) at the front and side sections with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails.
Assemble the two roof-slope side panels (D), drilling the entrance hole and dowel-perch hole in one before attaching.
Add the beveled wall panels (E, F, G, H, I) around the nesting compartments per the cut list, gluing and nailing each in place.
Attach the two hinged roof/door panels (J) with hinges so the interior can be opened for cleaning; insert the dowel perch.
Finish or leave natural; mount with appropriate outdoor hardware.

plans.title: Simple Bat House
plans.dimensions: Back panel 16-1/2in, sides 20in, front 18-19in, top 19-1/2in (see cut list)

cut_list.use: front (upper); 1/4in gap between front slats for ventilation

construction_notes: 1/8in deep grooves cut into back panel interior face (bat grip surface)
cedar or weather-resistant lumber recommended
no paint/stain recommended - most finishes are toxic to bats
plans.original_description: A single-chamber bat roost box built from three board widths (1x10, 1x3, 1x6), with a grooved back panel that gives bats grip to climb, and a narrow front-slat gap for ventilation.
original_steps: Cut two 1x10 back boards to 16-1/2"; join edge-to-edge with pocket screws and glue to form the back panel.
Cut shallow, parallel grooves (about 1/8" deep) across the inside face of the back panel for bat grip.
Cut and attach the two 1x3 side boards (20") flush to the back panel edges with glue and screws.
Attach the 1x10 and 1x6 front boards, leaving a 1/4" gap between them for ventilation.
Cut and center the 1x6 top board (19-1/2") flush to the back, securing with glue and screws.
Sand all edges smooth; leave unfinished or use a non-toxic exterior treatment (avoid paint/stain).
Mount high on a wall or tree, in a spot with morning sun exposure.

plans.title: Floor Standing Upholstered Headboard
plans.dimensions: Buildable up to 96in wide x 48in tall; shown at queen/king size in source photos
materials: 1-1/4in screws, brad nails, or staples (attaching panel to frame)
cut_list: 4 - 2x4 @ desired headboard height minus 3in, up to 45in
2 - 2x4 cleats, cut to size, for mounting
1 - 1/4in plywood/paneling panel, cut to frame size
plans.original_description: An upholstered headboard built like a small stud-wall frame from 2x4s, faced with thin plywood, padded, and wrapped in fabric. It stands on the floor rather than attaching to a bed frame, so it works behind any bed style.
original_steps: Build a rectangular 2x4 frame sized to the desired headboard width and height, fastened with 2-3/4" screws.
Attach mounting cleats to the back of the frame for securing to a wall or bed frame later.
Fasten a 1/4" plywood or paneling panel to the front of the frame with glue and 1-1/4" fasteners.
Layer padding (batting, foam, or old comforters) over the panel and staple it to the back of the frame.
Stretch fabric over the padded panel: staple the bottom edge first, then pull taut and staple the remaining sides, folding corners neatly.
Secure the finished headboard to the wall (French cleat) or to the bed frame for stability.

plans.title: Homestead Bench
plans.dimensions: 5ft wide x 15in deep x 18in tall
cut_list: 3 - 2x4 @ 13-1/2", both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (top cleats)
4 - 2x4 @ 15-1/4", both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to short point, ends parallel (legs)
2 - 2x4 @ 11-1/2", both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (legs)
2 - 2x4 @ 7-5/8", both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (legs)
plans.extra: Part of a matching furniture collection (console table, end table/nightstand, coffee table) on the source site
plans.original_description: An intermediate indoor/outdoor bench with a hidden-fastener look, angled 2x4 legs, and a 2x8 top; part of a matching furniture collection.
original_steps: Cut the two 2x8 top boards to 60" and all leg/cleat/stretcher pieces per the cut list, noting the 10-degree angled ends on the top cleats and legs.
Assemble each leg pair (long + short angled 2x4 pieces) into an angled leg assembly; repeat for both ends.
Join the two leg assemblies with the 2x4 bottom stretcher (52") for stability.
Attach the 2x8 top boards to the leg assemblies using the angled top cleats, positioned to keep fasteners hidden from view.
Sand and finish with paint or stain suited to indoor or outdoor use.

plans.title: Small Mason Bee House
plans.dimensions: Small by design (deliberately smaller than typical multi-species 'bee hotels'); nesting holes 6-8in deep
materials: exterior spray paint (blue or purple recommended; avoid red/dark tones)
cut_list: 2 - roof panels, 22.5-degree bevel on top edge
2 - side panels, top edge beveled to match roof
1 - laminated 2x4 nesting block, drilled with 5/16in (or slightly larger) holes, 6-8in deep
construction_notes: Nesting block should be removable/replaceable, not glued in, so it can be swapped every 1-2 years
Hole diameter and depth affect which bee species/sex use the house - vary rather than standardize
Overhang keeps rain off the nesting holes
Paint exterior only; leave interior nesting wood bare
plans.original_description: A small wall-mounted mason-bee house with an angled roof and a removable drilled nesting block, deliberately kept small (rather than one large multi-species 'bee hotel') to match how solitary bees actually nest in the wild.
original_steps: Cut two roof panels with a 22.5-degree bevel on the top edge so they meet at a peak.
Cut two side panels, beveling the top edge to match the roof angle.
Cut a bottom panel and a back panel from scrap stock or plywood.
Glue the sides, bottom, and back together into a box, then attach the beveled roof panels on top.
Laminate scrap 2x4 stock into a block sized to the depth of the housing, then drill 5/16in (or slightly larger) holes 6-8in deep to serve as a removable nesting insert.
Paint the exterior only (blue or purple recommended; avoid red/dark tones); leave the nesting block unfinished.
Mount with the overhang facing morning sun; replace the nesting block every 1-2 years.

plans.title: Laminated Canoe Paddle
plans.dimensions: No fixed cut list - shaft stock milled to 1.25in (32mm) square/thick; finished blade shaped to about 1/4in (6mm) thick; overall length sized to builder's own chin height
materials: assorted hardwood strips for lamination (e.g. maple, cherry, teak, padauk - contrasting species optional)
optional: fiberglass cloth + epoxy for tip reinforcement
tools: router with roundover bit (optional, for shaft)
plans.estimated_time: Roughly 4 days part-time, per source author's account
construction_notes: Shaft cross-section is the one fixed dimension: 1.25in (32mm) square before shaping down to round
Paddle length rule of thumb: approximately equal to the builder's own chin height
Grip width rule of thumb: hands a bit more than shoulder-width apart on the shaft
Referenced source material: 'Canoecraft' by Ted Moores (book, not reproduced here)
No numeric blade-shape pattern - traced from a prior paddle/template and shaped by eye to a target thickness
plans.original_description: A canoe paddle built up from laminated strips of contrasting hardwoods rather than carved from a single board: strips are milled to a common thickness, glued into one blank, then bandsawn to a traditional ottertail profile and hand-shaped.
original_steps: Mill all lumber strips to a consistent 1.25in (32mm) thickness before laminating - this becomes the shaft's cross-section.
Glue up the strips into a single blank in one full-length clamping session (the whole paddle needs even clamping pressure at once).
Trace a paddle outline onto the blank - sized to the builder's own chin height, with a grip-to-shaft spacing a bit wider than shoulder width - and cut the profile on a bandsaw.
Mark a centerline down both faces of the blade, then resaw close to the shoulder lines on the bandsaw to remove bulk waste before hand-shaping.
Hand-shape the blade to about 1/4in (6mm) thickness with a block plane, checking constantly against the centerline; round the shaft with a router roundover bit and/or block plane.
Sand progressively to a smooth finish; optionally reinforce the blade tip with fiberglass cloth and epoxy.
Apply several light coats of spar urethane, sanding lightly between coats.
plans.note: This source has no numeric cut list - it's a narrative/pattern-based build. Facts above are the only measurable data available; the shape itself is not quantified in the source.

plans.title: Outdoor Grill Cart
plans.dimensions: 30in tall (portable-grill height) x 6ft long x 2-1/2ft wide; length/width adjustable
construction_notes: Use a heat-safe surface under the grill; follow the grill's own clearance instructions
plans.original_description: A rolling outdoor grill prep cart at counter height, with a slatted cedar top and a full slatted lower shelf, mounted on caster wheels so it can move around the patio.
original_steps: Cut the four 4x4 legs to 23-3/4in and drill pocket holes in both ends of all 2x4 pieces.
Build two identical end-frame assemblies by joining the short (21in) 2x4 pieces between pairs of legs with pocket screws.
Join the two end frames with the long (65in) 2x4 aprons to form the full-length frame.
Add the 2x4 center supports (24-1/2in) across the frame to carry the top and shelf boards.
Cover the top and lower shelf openings with the 5-1/2in wide cedar boards, spaced evenly.
Attach caster wheels to the bottom of the legs.
Finish with an exterior penetrating stain.

plans.title: Large Three-Sided Outdoor Bar
plans.dimensions: About 7ft wide x almost 4ft deep x 40in high
construction_notes: No pocket holes required; countertop boards oriented bark-side up so water sheds instead of cupping
Finish like an exterior deck or fence
plans.original_description: A large three-sided outdoor serving bar, framed in 2x4 with cedar-fence-picket siding on the front and two sides, topped with a 2x12 countertop and an internal notched shelf, built without pocket-hole joinery for a quicker build.
original_steps: Cut all 2x4 framing and fence-picket siding pieces per the cut list.
Build the front wall frame first (top/bottom rails, vertical studs, horizontal blocking), fastening with screws.
Attach the front cedar fence-picket siding, evenly spaced, with brad nails.
Build the two side wall frames separately, then attach them to the front wall.
Cover both side frames with fence-picket siding.
Fit and attach trim pieces to the sides, then the front.
Notch the 2x12 inside shelf to fit the frame with a jigsaw, then screw it in place.
Assemble the 2x12 countertop boards with pocket screws (bark side up for drainage), then attach the completed top to the bar frame.
Finish as you would an outdoor deck or fence.

plans.title: Charlie Nightstand
plans.dimensions: Narrow, deep profile; carcass 17in wide; drawer opening 14in wide
plans.extra: Matching Charlie Dresser plans also available on the source site
plans.original_description: A narrow-but-deep plywood nightstand with a face frame, one large drawer on slides, and an open bottom shelf, topped with 1x8 boards edged in cove moulding.
original_steps: Cut the plywood carcass sides, bottom shelf, and back panel, plus the solid-wood face frame, trim, and tabletop boards, per the cut list.
Join the bottom shelf between the two side panels with pocket screws and glue to form the basic box.
Add the 1x3 back support with pocket holes, then attach the plywood back panel to square up the carcass.
Build a face frame from 1x2 stock sized to match the carcass width and attach it to the front of the box.
Add base trim around the bottom and drawer-slide support cleats inside the carcass.
Build the drawer box from 1x4 stock with a plywood bottom, mount it on drawer slides inset behind the face frame, then attach a 1x8 drawer face with a consistent reveal gap.
Assemble the tabletop boards, attach to the carcass with a consistent overhang on front and sides, and finish the exposed edges with cove moulding.
Fill nail holes, sand with the grain, then paint or stain.

plans.title: Modern Platform Bed Frame
plans.dimensions: Sized about 1in larger than the mattress on sides/bottom for a platform feel; available Twin, Full, Queen, King, California King
cut_list: Side rails: 4 - 2x6 @ 11-1/4in; 2 - 2x6 @ mattress length + 1in; 2 - 1x12 @ mattress length - 2in; 2 - 1x3 @ mattress length - 5in (cleats)
Inner support/cleats: 1 - 2x4 @ mattress length - 2-3/4in (notched 3/4in x 3-1/2in at one end); 3 - 2x4 @ 12in; 12 - 1x3 @ mattress width - 9in (Twin uses no center support system)
Per-size cut dimensions (Twin/Full/Queen/King/Cal King) are fully tabulated in the source using the formulas above
plans.extra: Matching headboard plan (floor-standing-headboard, also in this dataset) attaches to this frame
plans.original_description: A modern enclosed-base platform bed frame with hidden pocket-hole joinery, built as separate side-rail and footboard sections that are small enough to carry into a bedroom individually, then assembled in place; the same cut-list formula scales from Twin to California King.
original_steps: Cut the footboard, side rail, and inner-support pieces to the dimensions for the chosen mattress size, using the length/width formulas in the cut list.
Build the footboard: attach the shorter 1x12 boards to the main footboard 1x12 with pocket holes on the outside, then cap with the 2x6 on top.
Build each side rail as a 2x6 frame, then screw a 1x12 inside the frame (this becomes an enclosed toe-kick look); build two side rails.
Attach the two side rails to the footboard at floor level from the inside.
Add the inner cleats and center support inside the frame to carry the mattress slats (Twin size skips the center support).
Place the 1x3 cleats on top of the side rails and center support for the slats to rest on.
Attach the matching headboard to the assembled frame with screws.

plans.title: Ombre Striped Cutting Board
plans.dimensions: No fixed cut list - built from scrap strips of varying width, planed to a consistent 1in thickness
tools: scroll saw or jigsaw (optional, for a non-rectangular shape)
construction_notes: Test each strip's finished color with a dab of oil before final arrangement - raw wood tone differs a lot from oiled tone
Sand progressively from ~40-80 grit up to 220 grit
plans.original_description: A striped hardwood cutting board built from scrap-wood offcuts arranged in a graduated (ombre) color order, with an optional scroll-sawn teardrop shape instead of a plain rectangle.
original_steps: Rip scrap hardwood into strips of varying width and plane all strips to a consistent 1in thickness.
Dab a little finishing oil on each strip to preview its true finished color, then arrange the strips in the desired gradient order.
Glue and clamp all strips edge-to-edge in one glue-up using pipe clamps.
After the glue cures, scrape off squeeze-out, then sand progressively from coarse to fine grit (a belt sander speeds up initial leveling).
Optional: trace and cut a non-rectangular shape (e.g. a teardrop) with a scroll saw or jigsaw, then drill a hanging hole with a 1.5in forstner bit.
Optional: round over the edges with a router and roundover bit.
Finish with food-safe butcher-block oil.

plans.title: L-Shaped Corner Desk
plans.dimensions: L-shaped, built in two sections that join on site (sized to fit through a standard door individually)
materials: 3 - 2x4 x 8ft boards (ripped to 1-1/4in square strips for the base)
construction_notes: 2x4 stock is ripped down to a slim 1-1/4in square profile for a lighter-looking base than standard 2x4 legs
Assembled as two separate L-halves, then joined in place, so it can fit through doorways
plans.original_description: An L-shaped corner desk built from ripped-down 2x4 and 1x6 stock, using a slim 1-1/4in square frame for a lighter look than typical desk legs, assembled as two separate halves that join on site.
original_steps: Glue up the wider desktop boards into two oversized panels (one long run, one medium run for the L) and let cure overnight before trimming to final size.
Rip 2x4 stock down to 1-1/4in square strips for all base framing.
Cut all base pieces (legs, short base, long/medium frame, supports) per the cut list, joining frame corners with pocket holes.
Assemble each half of the L-shaped base separately so each piece can fit through a doorway.
Sand and finish: paint the base, stain the desktop panels.
Move both base halves into place, join them together, then attach the desktop panels from underneath through the support rails with wood screws.

plans.title: Plywood Jewelry Box
plans.dimensions: Roughly 8-3/8in x 5in footprint, two-tier (lid box + base box)
plans.original_description: A small two-tier plywood jewelry box (a shallow lid tray sitting on a deeper base), built entirely from 1/2in plywood with pocket-hole joinery, ironed-on edge banding to hide the plywood edges, and hinged at the back.
original_steps: Cut all plywood pieces to size for both the top (lid) box and bottom box.
Drill two pocket holes along each side of the top and bottom panels using a micro pocket-hole jig sized for thin stock.
Assemble the bottom box: attach the four side pieces to the bottom panel with pocket screws, reinforcing with brad nails if needed.
Assemble the top (lid) box the same way.
Iron on edge banding over exposed plywood edges, trim with a utility knife, and sand smooth.
Fill any tear-out or exposed pocket holes with wood filler, sand smooth, then paint or stain.
Attach hinges and a latch to join the lid to the base.
Optional: glue in a small mirror and jewelry inserts.

plans.title: 4ft Rectangular Cedar Planter Box
plans.dimensions: 4ft rectangular planter, mitered trim cap
materials: plastic planter liner (not itemized in shopping list but used in build)

cut_list.use: bottom slats (last piece cut to fit)

cut_list.size: 1-3/4in x 48in, 45-degree cuts both ends opposite direction
1-3/4in x 12in, 45-degree cuts both ends opposite direction

construction_notes: Drill 3/4in drainage holes through the bottom slats
Staple plastic planter liner to the inside bottom and sides before use
plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download of this plan
plans.original_description: A 4-foot rectangular cedar-fence-board planter box with a mitered trim cap, built as a simple box frame with internal bottom supports and a slatted floor, lined with plastic sheeting for drainage.
original_steps: Cut the eight corner-support pieces (A) and assemble the box corners with exterior glue and finish nails.
Cut and attach the front/back panels (B) and side panels (C) to the corner supports to form the box walls.
Cut and install the bottom supports (D) and front/back braces (F) inside the box to carry the floor slats.
Cut the bottom floor slats (E) to fit and nail them onto the bottom supports, trimming the last piece as needed.
Cut the mitered trim pieces (G front/back, H sides) with 45-degree corners and attach around the top edge as a cap.
Drill 3/4in drainage holes through the floor slats.
Staple plastic planter liner inside the box over the bottom and sides.
Sand and finish, or leave bare for a natural weathered look.

plans.title: Classic 6ft Picnic Table
plans.dimensions: 6ft picnic table
materials: 2x6 boards (top, legs, seat support, seats - various lengths)
3in x 3/8in diameter carriage bolts with nuts/washers (16)
construction_notes: Bolted (not just screwed) with carriage bolts through the leg/brace joints for a stronger, semi-knockdown frame
plans.original_description: A classic 6-foot A-frame picnic table with a five-board top, two bench seats, and angled 2x6 legs braced by cleats and diagonal braces, bolted together for a sturdier frame than an all-screw build.
original_steps: Cut all tabletop, seat, leg, brace, batten, and cleat pieces per the cut list.
Assemble each A-frame leg pair (2x6 legs + cleat) with carriage bolts.
Attach the seat supports across the two leg assemblies to form the main frame.
Fasten the battens across the underside of the tabletop boards to hold the five top boards together, then attach the assembled top to the frame.
Attach the two seat boards on each side to the seat supports.
Add the diagonal braces between the legs and seat supports for rigidity, securing with carriage bolts and deck screws.
Sand and finish with an exterior-rated stain or paint.

plans.title: Workbench with Hutch
plans.dimensions: Not stated numerically beyond cut list; optimized to use full plywood sheet lengths with minimal waste
tools: circular saw (with speed square/guide) or miter saw
plans.original_description: A garage/shop workbench with an open-back hutch/shelving unit above the work surface, framed entirely from 2x4s with a 1/2in plywood top and shelves, sized to use plywood sheets efficiently.
original_steps: Build the back frame flat on the floor, keeping all edges flush, using the longer 2-3/4in screws.
Build the front frame the same way, omitting the bottom two rails so the main workbench top stays open at the bottom.
Join the front and back frames with the 13in blocking pieces to set the depth.
Build the bottom shelf frame separately, then attach it to the back rail and level it against the front frame.
Attach the workbench tabletop support rails, checking level as you go.
Attach the main center support beneath the tabletop span.
Stand the short legs into place and fasten with screws and glue; add the front rail.
Cut and attach the plywood panels to the top and shelf using the shorter screws.

plans.title: Nesting Cutting Boards (+ Egg Holder)
plans.dimensions: No fixed cut list - shape is freehand-traced/templated, not dimensioned
tools: orbital sander or router with roundover bit
spray adhesive + painter's tape (for template transfer)
construction_notes: First board is cut freehand, then used as the physical template to trace the second board so the pair nests together
Companion egg-holder project reuses the same taped-template + jigsaw + hole-saw method
plans.original_description: A pair of nested cutting boards cut from two contrasting wood species so they store flush together like interlocking puzzle pieces, plus a companion egg-holder tray using the same template-transfer method.
original_steps: Sketch a freehand cutting-board silhouette with a wide handle opening onto the first board blank.
Cut the first board out with a jigsaw, drilling a starter hole first if the handle opening is fully enclosed.
Lay the finished first board against the edge of the second blank and trace along it, then complete the second board's outline so the two nest together.
Cut the second board to match, then sand or rout-round the edges of both boards.
Finish both boards with food-safe mineral oil.

Optional companion project: tape a printed egg-holder template to a board, spray-adhesive it in place, jigsaw-cut along the untaped edges, drill the egg wells with a hole saw, then remove the template.

plans.title: No-Power-Tools Spice Drawer Organizer
plans.dimensions: Fully parametric - frame width set by the drawer, frame depth/spacing set by test-fitting a spice jar
tools: fine saw and miter box (or miter saw)
construction_notes: No power tools required
Angle of the spice jars is tuned by adjusting the spacing between the two long strips before cutting the connecting pieces
plans.original_description: A no-power-tools spice-jar rack built from thin wood strips assembled into simple slanted frames that hold spice jars at a readable angle inside a kitchen drawer.
original_steps: Measure the width of the drawer.

Cut two long strips to the drawer's width, place them in the drawer, and test-fit a spice jar on top of them to find a spacing that tilts the jar to a readable angle without blocking the drawer from closing.
Measure the gap between the two long strips and cut two short connecting pieces to that length.
Sand all pieces, then glue the short pieces between the long pieces to form a rectangular frame; clamp until dry.
Repeat to build as many frames as needed to fill the drawer width.
Cut and insert loose spacer strips between the last frame and the drawer side to keep everything from shifting (no glue needed).
Load the drawer with spice jars.

plans.title: Built-In Style Bookcases
plans.dimensions: No numeric cut list published free (full cut list is behind a paid PDF plan on the source site)
construction_notes: Shelves are adjustable via a column of shelf-pin holes, not fixed
Doors are a simple frame with a routed groove holding a 1/4in plywood panel
No trim on any side that will sit flush against a wall
plans.original_description: A pair of built-in-style bookcases with adjustable shelf-pin shelving and simple frame-and-panel cabinet doors, trimmed out and braced into the wall so they read as part of the room rather than furniture pushed into a corner.
original_steps: Build each bookcase's box frame (sides, top, bottom, and a fixed shelf that doubles as structure) from 3/4in plywood, joined with pocket screws and glue.
Drill a column of evenly spaced shelf-pin holes on the inside front and back of each side panel for adjustable shelving.
Cut adjustable shelves from 3/4in plywood to fit and sand them.
Build simple frame-and-panel doors: assemble a frame, rout a groove on the inside edge, and glue in a 1/4in plywood panel.
Add trim across the front and any exposed side, filling nail holes; skip trim on any side that will sit flush against a wall.
Paint all components (sides, shelves, doors) before installation.
To install: remove base moulding along the back and side walls where the case will sit, slide the case into place, then brace it to the wall studs with brackets so it can't tip or shift.
Hang the cabinet doors and drop the shelves onto the shelf pins.

plans.title: Minimalist Modern Tall Dresser
plans.dimensions: Tall, narrow-drawer dresser sized for accessories/smaller items rather than deep storage (see cut list)
materials: 1 full panel 3/4in plywood, ripped into 16in wide strips
1 half panel (4x4ft) 1/4in plywood, ripped into 16in wide strips
cut_list: 5 - 1x10 @ 20-3/4in (drawer faces, ripped to 8-1/2in with inside 45-degree bevel)
plans.extra: Part of Ana White's Pottery Barn Cayman-inspired furniture collection (matching bed, nightstands, 6-drawer dresser)
plans.original_description: A minimalist tall dresser built mostly from ripped-down plywood strips and 1x trim rather than dimensional lumber, with five drawers on euro-style slides and beveled drawer faces for a shadow-gap look.
original_steps: Rip the plywood sheets into 16in wide strips, then crosscut to the side-panel and top-panel sizes; rip the 1x stock, cutting the longest pieces first.
Build the front frame from 1x3 strips with brad nails and glue, checking it for square by comparing diagonal measurements.
Attach the remaining 1x3 supports to the two side plywood panels (mirrored left/right), then attach the front frame to the assembled sides.
Attach 1x2 trim around the bottom of the case.
Build the top: attach trim to the top panel's ends and front, glue it in place on the case, and nail through all available attachment points.
Attach trim to the front and back edges of the case, flush to the outside.
Build the five drawer boxes from 1x6 stock and install them on euro-style drawer slides.
Rip the 1x10 drawer faces to width with an inside 45-degree bevel on the top edge, attach to the drawer boxes with brad nails, then reinforce from behind with screws.

plans.title: Low-Rise Candle Holder
plans.dimensions: 16in base length; small pedestal stand for 3 candles
materials: wood glue or joinery of choice (pocket holes, dowels, nails)
plans.original_description: A small scrap-wood pedestal stand that lifts a row of three candles up on a raised base, built from two simple leg assemblies joined by a single long top board.
original_steps: Cut the base, two braces, and four posts to size, then sand all pieces.
Build two leg assemblies: join a brace between two posts on each end, with the brace positioned about 1in below the top of the posts.
Apply glue to the top of each brace, then slide the base board down between the posts to rest on the braces, positioned about 2in in from each end.
Clamp and let the glue cure.
Finish with a wipe-on oil or preferred finish.
Set candles or candle holders along the raised base.

plans.title: Homestead End Table
plans.dimensions: Small end table; can double as a stool or extra seating
cut_list: 2 - 2x4 @ 13-1/2in, both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (tabletop cleats)
4 - 2x4 @ 17-1/4in, both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to short point, ends parallel (legs)
2 - 2x4 @ 12-1/4in, both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (legs)
2 - 2x4 @ 7-5/8in, both ends 10 degrees off square, long point to long point, ends not parallel (legs)
tags: Living Room Tables and Accent Table Plans
plans.extra: Part of Ana White's Homestead Collection (matching bench also in this dataset); this cut list is written to build two at once
plans.original_description: A small homestead-style end table (also usable as a stool or nightstand) with angled 2x4 legs, a thick 2x8 top, and an open bottom shelf; part of a matching furniture collection built with pocket-hole joinery.
original_steps: Cut all tabletop, leg, and shelf pieces per the cut list, noting the 10-degree angled ends on the top cleats and legs.
Lay out the 2x8 tabletop boards and center the angled 2x4 cleats across the underside, leaving a 1in overhang at each end; attach with screws and glue.
Build two leg assemblies from the angled 2x4 leg pieces, using pocket holes to hide fasteners (or self-tapping screws if no pocket-hole jig is available).
Attach the leg assemblies to the underside of the tabletop cleats.
Clamp the bottom shelf boards in place and fasten from underneath with screws (and nails/glue if available).
Sand and finish with stain or paint.

plans.title: Face-Frame Buffet Table
plans.dimensions: No single overall-dimension summary given; built from a 2-section face frame with a center divider
construction_notes: Doors left a 1/8in gap on all sides of their openings
Bottom of side frames sits 2.5in below the face frame bottom (creates a base reveal)
No numeric cut list published free - dimensions above are the ones stated in the build text; full cut list is behind the source's separate free printable
plans.original_description: A face-frame buffet cabinet with a plywood carcass, a center divider splitting the interior into two sections, flat-panel doors, and a planked 2x6 top, built almost entirely with pocket-hole joinery.
original_steps: Rip 2x4 stock to 3in wide and build two face frames (front and back) with pocket holes, glue, and pocket screws.
Build two matching side panels from 3/4in plywood with pocket holes down the back edge, trimmed on the front edge with 1x4 stock.
Attach the two side panels to the back face frame, flush at the top on both sides.
Cut a 3/4in plywood bottom panel and attach it to the back face frame and side panels with pocket screws and glue.
Cut a 3/4in plywood center divider, slightly shorter than the sides, and attach it to the base and the face frame's center leg.
Attach the front face frame to the sides and divider, keeping outside edges and the top flush.
Build two flat-panel doors from 1/2in plywood, leaving a consistent 1/8in gap around each opening, and trim each door's face with mitered strips.
Build the top by ripping 2x6 boards to 5in wide, joining them edge-to-edge with pocket holes, then attach the assembled top to the cabinet with long wood screws through the face frame.
Cut a 1/4in plywood back panel and attach it with glue and staples or nails.
Paint the cabinet and doors, stain the top, then install hinges and door pulls.

plans.title: Raised Garden Bed with Hinged Cover
plans.dimensions: Height fixed at 14in (post height); length/width adjustable to the builder's chosen footprint (source used 8ft x 4ft)
materials: black plastic deer/bird netting, about 7ft x 100ft roll
cut_list: 2x6 front/back panels cut to chosen bed length
2x6 side panels cut to chosen bed width
2x2 cover frame pieces cut to match the top opening
construction_notes: Landscape fabric doubled up and stapled to the inside walls/bottom before adding soil
Leave part of the netting cover open so pollinators can still reach the plants
plans.original_description: A pressure-treated raised garden bed with a hinged 2x2 frame cover wrapped in bird/deer netting, sized to whatever footprint you choose by adjusting the length of the 2x6 sides.
original_steps: Cut six 4x4 posts to 14in tall (this sets the bed height).
Cut the 2x6 boards to your chosen bed length for the front and back panels.
Assemble the front and back panels by screwing the 2x6 boards to the 4x4 posts (one post at each end, one centered) with exterior screws.
Cut the 2x6 side boards to your chosen bed width and screw them to the end posts to close the box.
Clear and level the ground inside the bed, then staple a doubled layer of landscape fabric to the inside walls and across the bottom.
Fill with garden soil and compost/manure, mix well, and let it settle before planting.
Build a lightweight 2x2 frame sized to the top opening for the cover, pre-drilling screw holes to prevent splitting, and reinforce corners with metal corner braces.
Staple bird/deer netting over the cover frame, leaving part uncovered so pollinators can still reach the plants, then attach the cover to the bed with hinges.

plans.title: 4x8 Chicken Coop
plans.dimensions: 4ft x 8ft footprint
cut_list: Rafters: 10 - 2x4 @ 6ft 6in, 40-degree angle cuts both ends same direction (6 with a bird's-mouth notch)
Roof deck: 4 - 2x4 @ 5ft 9in; 4 - 1/2in plywood 4x8ft sheets, cut to size
Doors: 7 - 2x4 @ 8ft cut to size; door frame pieces 2 - 2x4 @ 3ft 3-1/2in, 2 - 2x4 @ 3ft 9in (notched)
plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download; nest box is a separate linked plan not included in this material list
plans.original_description: A 4x8 chicken coop with a raised 2x6 floor frame, stick-framed walls, a nest box, and a shed-style roof with trusses cut at 40 degrees, sided in T1-11 exterior panels.
original_steps: Build the floor frame from 2x6 lumber per the cut list, squaring it up on a level site.
Frame each wall section (left, inside divider, right, back, front) separately from 2x4s per the cut list, then stand and fasten all wall frames to the floor frame.

Cut and assemble the roof trusses from 2x4s with 40-degree angle cuts, adding a bird's-mouth notch on half of them where they sit on the wall top plate, and glue/screw in plywood gussets at the joints.
Install the roof trusses onto the wall frames.
Build and install the nest box, then side the walls with T1-11 panels, cutting door and mesh-door openings as you go.
Frame and hang the door(s), covering the wire-mesh door section with hardware cloth stapled in place.
Install the roof deck plywood, then roofing paper and shingles.
Staple wire mesh across the floor-frame opening for predator protection.

plans.title: Stackable 2x4 Sawhorses
plans.dimensions: Standard sawhorse height from 30in 2x4 legs; one horse built 2in longer than the other so the pair nests for storage
cut_list: 14 - 2x4 @ 30in (legs and I-beam caps, for a pair of sawhorses)
construction_notes: Optional 20-degree bevel on the feet improves stability on hard floors
Optional stretcher braces between the lower legs add anti-sway stability and can carry a removable (not glued) plywood shelf
To stack: build one I-beam 2in longer with legs near-flush to its ends, and the other with legs set 1in in from the ends
plans.original_description: A pair of stackable 2x4 sawhorses built from a sandwiched I-beam spine with angled legs, sized so one horse nests inside the other for storage, with no metal brackets required.
original_steps: Cut fourteen 30in lengths of 2x4 (enough for two I-beams and four legs each).
Build each I-beam by sandwiching one 30in board between two others, centered with a 1in overhang on each end, and screwing them together.
Angle four 30in legs under each I-beam's lip and screw each leg on, using a square to keep the legs square to the beam.
Check for wobble by driving one screw per leg first, setting the horse on a flat surface, then adjusting each leg before driving the rest of the screws.
To make the pair stack: build one I-beam 2in longer than standard with its legs mounted almost flush to the ends, and build the other with its legs set 1in in from the ends.
Optional: bevel the feet at 20 degrees for better stability on hard floors, and add stretcher braces between the legs (with a removable drop-in plywood shelf) for extra load capacity.

plans.title: Simple Plywood Tool Box
plans.dimensions: Entire box cut from a single 18in x 26in half-sheet of 1/2in plywood; no further numeric cut list published free
construction_notes: Panel sizes are shown only in the source's diagram/paid PDF, not in free text - dimensions here reflect only what's stated as plain text
plans.original_description: A simple five-piece plywood tool box (bottom, two sides, two ends) with a dowel-rod carry handle, sized to be cut entirely from one 18in x 26in half-sheet of plywood - a good first build for a beginner learning hand tools.
original_steps: Cut the bottom, two side panels, and two end panels from a single 18in x 26in sheet of 1/2in plywood.
Nail one side panel to the bottom panel with finish nails.
Nail the second side panel to the other edge of the bottom panel.
Nail both end panels between the two side panels to close the box.
Cut the 1-1/4in dowel rod to fit between the two end pieces above the box as a handle, and nail it in place through each end (two nails per side to keep it from twisting).
Sand and paint or finish as desired.

plans.title: Wooden Gear Wall Clock
plans.dimensions: Single board, about 12in square; must be at least 1/4in thick for the clock mechanism shaft
materials: 2x12 board (or any board at least 1/4in thick) cut to about 12in
construction_notes: Shape comes from a printable gear template, not a numeric cut list
plans.original_description: A wall clock shaped like a mechanical gear, cut from a single board using a printable template and finished with a standard quartz clock mechanism.
original_steps: Print a gear template and tape or spray-adhesive it to a board at least 1/4in thick.
Cut out the gear shape with a scroll saw or jigsaw.
Find the center and drill a hole sized to the clock mechanism's shaft with a Forstner bit.
Sand the cut edges and faces smooth.
Paint or stain as desired.
Install the clock mechanism through the center hole.

plans.title: Circle Cutting Jig for Circular Saw
plans.dimensions: Adjustable to any circle size within the plywood length used
plans.original_description: A shop-made jig that lets a circular saw cut clean circles, sized by drilling a pivot hole at the jig's radius.
original_steps: Cut a piece of 1/4in plywood about 12in wide and as long as your largest needed radius.
Attach a 1x2 to one edge with glue and fasteners, leaving a 1-1/2in overhang past the 1x2.
Trim the overhang flush with your circular saw's base plate, using the 1x2 as a guide.
Find the plywood's width-wise center, measure out the desired radius, and drill a pivot hole matching your saw's blade offset.
Pin the pivot hole at the center of your workpiece and swing the saw around it to cut the circle, clamping the jig if it rotates.

plans.title: Stacked-Edge Plywood Picture Frame
plans.dimensions: Adjustable; source built an 8in x 10in frame
construction_notes: The visual feature is the exposed stacked-ply edge grain, so plywood choice matters more than usual
plans.original_description: A picture frame built from ripped plywood strips that showcase the stacked plywood-edge grain as the design feature, rabbeted to hold glass and a backing.
original_steps: Rip the plywood into strips (about 3/4in wide) on the table saw - use more strips for a wider frame.
Rout or table-saw a rabbet along the inside back edge of each strip, sized to your glass and backing thickness.
Miter the strips to length for your chosen photo size.
Glue and clamp the mitered corners together, checking for square.
Drop in the glass, photo, and backing, then attach backing clips and a sawtooth hanger.

plans.title: Folding Guitar Stand
plans.dimensions: Blank width 9-1/2in; boards 36in long
plans.original_description: A folding guitar stand with a curved silhouette, built from glued-up 1x10 boards and a dowel-rod pivot, designed to fold flat for travel or storage.
original_steps: Join boards edge-to-edge to get a 9-1/2in wide blank, then true up one edge on a table saw.
Trace and cut the curved stand profile with a jigsaw or bandsaw.
Drill a hole sized to the 3/4in dowel rod for the folding pivot point.
Cut and drill the small latch piece that holds the stand open, and attach it with a screw pivot.
Sand progressively (150 then 220 grit), apply felt strip tape to guitar-contact points, and finish.

plans.title: Three-Board Scrap Wood Napkin Holder
plans.dimensions: Small; example cut a 1x6 into 6in, 3in, and 4in pieces
plans.original_description: A simple three-board scrap-wood napkin holder glued into a U-shaped cradle.
original_steps: Cut a 1x6 board into a base piece and two end pieces (6in/3in/4in, or adjust to taste).
Sand all pieces before assembly - the inside corners are hard to reach once glued.
Glue the two end pieces to the base piece to form a U-shaped cradle, clamping until dry.
Finish with stain, paint, or polyurethane.

plans.title: Scrap Wood Catch-All Tray
plans.dimensions: No fixed cut list - shape is freehand-traced, boards no more than 3/4in thick
materials: wood glue (CA glue optional to speed cure)
plans.original_description: A scrap-wood catch-all tray cut in any freehand shape (oval, animal, monogram) entirely with a jigsaw, no CNC or router required.
original_steps: Glue up enough scrap boards (no more than 3/4in thick) to cover your desired tray footprint.
Once dry, trace your chosen shape onto the panel.
Cut the outline with a jigsaw (scroll saw or bandsaw also work).
Sand the edges and faces smooth.
Finish as desired.

plans.title: Wooden Salt and Pepper Shakers
plans.dimensions: Small; built from 2x2 stock or glued-up 1x2 stock
materials: tight-grained hardwood (maple, walnut, cherry, or acacia) - 2x2 or glued 1x2 stock
construction_notes: Silicone stoppers recommended over cork - cork was hard to remove in the source's build
plans.original_description: A pair of wooden salt and pepper shakers made from small hardwood blanks, each sealed at the bottom with a cork or silicone stopper.
original_steps: Choose tight-grained hardwood blanks (2x2 stock, or glue up 1x2 stock) sized for one salt shaker and one pepper shaker.
Drill a cavity into the top of each blank sized to hold the seasoning.
Drill smaller dispensing holes through the top/cap in your chosen pattern.
Drill a stopper hole in the bottom sized to your cork or silicone stopper.
Sand and finish with a food-safe finish.
Insert the stopper in the bottom to close each shaker.

plans.title: Mobile Shop Router Table
plans.dimensions: Base 24in deep x 36in high with 35in legs
construction_notes: Each leg is two 2x4s dadoed and paired to form a solid 3in x 3in leg
Top is doubled 3/4in plywood with laminate surface for smoothness
plans.original_description: A mobile shop-built router table with a 2x4 base on casters, a doubled 3/4in plywood top with a laminate surface, and t-track slots for featherboards and fences.
original_steps: Build the 2x4 base (24in deep x 36in high, 35in legs), dadoing each leg pair 3/4in deep x 3in wide so two 2x4s combine into a solid 3in x 3in leg.
Glue and screw the base together, then attach the safety switch and casters.
Build the top from two layers of 3/4in plywood glued together, laminated on top, with dados cut for the t-track.
Rout the opening for the router insert plate and install the plate.
Install the t-track sections, then build an adjustable fence from plywood with knobs, t-track bolts, and carriage bolts to lock it down.

plans.title: Shop-Made Corner Clamp
plans.dimensions: Clamps stock up to about 3in wide (scalable larger or smaller)
construction_notes: The counterbored bolt slot on the jaw blocks must face DOWN against the base, not up - a noted mistake in the source build
plans.original_description: A shop-made corner clamp for gluing mitered frames, with a slotted plywood base that flexes to squeeze the miter joint closed rather than just pinning the corner.
original_steps: Cut the plywood base with slots that let the fixed jaw sections flex inward.
Glue the angled hardwood jaw blocks to the base, with the counterbored bolt slot facing down against the base.
Drill through the base and jaws for the carriage bolts.
Assemble with carriage bolts, washers, and wing nuts.
Clamp a mitered corner by tightening the wing nuts, drawing the flexing jaw sections together across the miter.

plans.title: Kid's First Toolbox
plans.dimensions: Small, kid-sized toolbox
plans.original_description: A small kid-sized toolbox with a curved dowel-rod handle, sized for a child's first hand tools.
original_steps: Cut two 1x4 pieces to 19-1/2in for the box sides.
Cut two 1x8 pieces to 9in for the ends, shape a curved or straight top corner on each, and cut the profile with a coping or scroll saw.
Drill a hole (sized to the dowel) about 1in below the top of each end piece for the handle.
Cut a 3/8in plywood bottom to 7-1/2in x 21in.
Glue the dowel handle into the two end pieces, then glue and nail the end pieces to the side pieces.
Glue and nail the plywood bottom to the assembled sides/ends, then sand smooth.

plans.title: Rolling Lumber Storage Cart
plans.dimensions: About 5ft x 5ft x 2ft
plans.original_description: A rolling lumber storage cart built entirely from 2x4s, with a slatted bottom frame and three upright rack frames on caster wheels.
original_steps: Build the bottom frame with star bit screws, two per joint, leaving a center opening sized to fit a 2x4.
Build three upright rack frames using star bit screws, two per joint.
Attach the upright frames to the bottom frame at 90 degrees, four screws per joint - level the bottom frame first, then use a level to set each upright.
Attach the upper tie-ins with pocket holes and pocket screws (or angled star bit screws from top and bottom).
Attach the caster wheels to the base, screwing into both the upright legs and the bottom frame.

plans.title: Media Tower Cabinet
plans.dimensions: Tall tower, six shelves, single door (hinge-side reversible)
plans.original_description: A tall media tower cabinet with six fixed shelves and a single door that can be hinged on either side, built from edge-banded 3/4in plywood with pocket-hole joinery.
original_steps: Cut the two side panels and apply edge banding to exposed edges before assembly.
Cut the bottom panel, drill pocket holes on its side edges, and attach to the sides with glue and pocket screws.
Cut and attach the bottom support strips beneath the bottom panel, into the sides.
Cut the back panel and attach it to the sides and bottom with pocket holes and glue.
Cut the five shelves and attach flush with the back, gluing and pocket-screwing through the sides and back.
Cut and attach the top panel.
Cut the door panel, edge-band it, and hang with two hinges (either side), adding a cabinet pull.
Finish with primer and paint, or stain and sealer.

plans.title: One-Board Magazine Rack
plans.dimensions: Built entirely from a single 1x3 board
construction_notes: Exact numeric cut list is in the source's separate downloadable plan, not in the free page text
plans.original_description: A wall-mounted magazine rack built entirely from a single 1x3 board, using pocket-hole joinery so magazine covers face outward for easy browsing.
original_steps: Cut the 1x3 board into a bottom, a back, and two angled side pieces sized to your preferred depth and height.
Drill pocket holes in the bottom and back pieces only.
Glue and pocket-screw the bottom to the two side pieces.
Glue and pocket-screw the back piece to the sides, angled to hold magazines facing out.
Fill any exposed holes with wood filler, sand, and paint.
Mount to the wall with hanging hardware or Command strips.

plans.title: Small Floating Shelf with Cutout Sides
plans.dimensions: Depth set by board choice: 7.5in deep with 1x8 boards, 5.5in deep with 1x6 boards
plans.original_description: A small floating shelf with a decorative cutout side profile, sized by choosing 1x8 (7.5in deep) or 1x6 (5.5in deep) boards.
original_steps: Decide the shelf's length and height, then cut the top and bottom boards to length and the side boards to height (adding 3/4in top and bottom once assembled).
Optional: trace and cut a decorative profile into the side pieces using a round object as a template.
Glue and nail the top and bottom boards between the two side pieces.
Sand and finish.
Mount to the wall with concealed floating-shelf hardware or a French cleat.

plans.title: Campaign-Style Storage Boxes
plans.dimensions: Per box: four 8in x 8in sides, one 8in x 7in bottom/lid
plans.estimated_time: About 30 minutes assembly per box (plus finish drying time)
plans.original_description: A set of small campaign-style storage boxes with brass flush ring pulls and optional corner braces, built from 1/2in board with pocket-hole joinery.
original_steps: Cut four 8in x 8in side boards and one 8in x 7in board (bottom/lid) per box from 1/2in birch or pine.
Drill pocket holes in two of the side boards.
Assemble the four sides into a box using pocket screws and glue, then attach the 8in x 7in panel.
Fill screw holes with wood filler, sand, then stain and seal with polyurethane.
Install the brass flush ring pull (and optional corner braces) on each box.

plans.title: Freestanding Garden Arbor
plans.dimensions: Not stated as a single overall figure; see cut list for part sizes
construction_notes: Freestanding - anchor with ground stakes rather than concrete to prevent tipping
Assembly order is by letter: A, then B, then C, etc.
plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download; matching Arbor Bench and Arbor Swing plans available
plans.original_description: A freestanding cedar/PT garden arbor built from 4x4 posts and half-ripped 2x4 crosspieces, anchored with ground stakes rather than concrete.
original_steps: Cut the four 4x4 posts (A) to 6ft.
Rip 2x4s in half lengthwise for the B pieces and cut to 4ft, 2ft 1-1/2in, and 9-13/16in per the cut list.
Assemble the posts and B pieces in order to build each side panel.
Cut and add the 4x4 top crosspieces (C) and 2x4 top rails (D) to join the two side panels into the arbor frame.
Rip and cut the E pieces for the top slats and attach across the top.
Anchor the arbor to the ground with stakes.

plans.title: Cedar Mailbox Post with Corbels
plans.dimensions: No fully itemized numeric cut list (documented as a photo build log rather than a numbered plan)
construction_notes: Cedar chosen for rot resistance and lighter weight vs. treated lumber
Finished with boiled linseed oil, not paint or stain
plans.original_description: A cedar mailbox post with decorative corbels, joined with half-lap joints and biscuits, finished with boiled linseed oil for a natural, weather-resistant look.
original_steps: Cut the 4x4 cedar posts to length, allowing extra at the ends to cut past any drying checks.
Lay out and cut half-lap joints where posts and corbels join.
Rout a 1/2in radius cove profile on the decorative corbel edges.
Glue joints with Titebond III and reinforce with biscuits.
Assemble the post, corbels, and mailbox mount with deck screws.
Finish with boiled linseed oil thinned with mineral spirits.

plans.title: PVC Window Box
plans.dimensions: Sized to match the window's width (example: 32in)
construction_notes: Built from PVC trim board instead of wood specifically to avoid rot
plans.original_description: A window box built from PVC trim board instead of wood to eliminate rot risk, sized to match the width of the window it sits under.
original_steps: Measure the window's width - this sets the box's overall length.
Cut all PVC pieces per the cut list, mitering the trim pieces as noted.
Glue the ripped bottom panel to one of the long side panels with PVC cement and finish nails, set in 3/4in from the edge.
Attach the two end panels with PVC cement and finish nails.
Attach the mitered trim pieces around the top and the cleat pieces to the back for mounting.
Mount to the house with Tapcon screws (brick) or wood screws (siding).

plans.title: Small Dog House
plans.dimensions: Floor deck 1ft 8-3/4in x 2ft 1-7/8in
plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download
plans.original_description: A small A-frame-roof dog house with a raised 2x4 floor frame on short rounded legs, sided entirely in T1-11 exterior panels cut from a single 4x8 sheet.
original_steps: Build the floor frame from 2x4s per the cut list, and attach four 6in 2x4 legs with rounded bottoms.
Cut the five siding panels (floor deck, two sides, front/back with 45-degree angled tops, roof) from one sheet of T1-11 siding.
Attach 2x2 cleats inside the siding panels so the deck screws have something to bite into.
Screw the floor deck siding to the floor frame.
Screw the side panels to the floor frame and to each other along the corners.
Attach the front and back panels between the sides.
Attach the two roof panels last, screwing through the siding into the 2x2 cleats.

plans.title: Planter Box with Attached Trellis
plans.dimensions: Not stated as a single overall figure; see cut list for part sizes
construction_notes: Drill drainage holes in the bottom boards before use
plans.original_description: A planter box with an attached lattice-slat trellis rising from the back, built from cedar boards on a 2x6 base with mitered 2x4 top trim.
original_steps: Cut the twelve cedar planter-side boards (A) to 13in and the connecting 1x2 pieces (B, C) to length, then assemble two side panels and a front/back panel.
Join all four planter walls together, then cut and attach the two cedar bottom boards (D), drilling drainage holes.
Cut the 2x6 base rails (E, F) and attach the assembled planter on top of them.
Cut and miter the 2x4 top trim pieces (G, H) at 45 degrees and attach around the top edge of the planter.
Cut the 2x2 trellis uprights (I, J, K) and 1x2 slats (L) and assemble the lattice trellis frame.
Attach the trellis frame to the back of the planter/base assembly.

plans.title: Small Square Farmhouse Dining Table
plans.dimensions: Legs spaced 20in apart, sized for one standard dining chair per side
construction_notes: Top attaches via z-clips to allow for wood expansion/contraction
X-stretcher legs get a notch (start 6in from bottom, up 2-1/2in) cut by hand with a saw and chisel or a jig-guided power tool
plans.original_description: A small square farmhouse dining table sized for one chair per side, with legs spaced 20in apart and an X-shaped lower stretcher joined with a half-lap for legroom.
original_steps: Cut four legs from 4x4 stock to 29-1/2in, optionally ripping to 3in x 3in (or 2-1/2in square) for a slimmer look.
Cut an angled notch in each leg (starting 6in from the bottom, running up 2-1/2in) to accept the X-stretcher.
Cut the skirt boards from 1x4 to 20in, groove each for z-clip attachment, then pocket-screw and glue between the legs.
Cut the X-stretcher pieces from 2x3 to 29-3/4in with a half-lap dado in the center of each so they interlock, glue together, then pocket-screw to the legs.
Attach the top boards to the leg assembly using z-clips.

plans.title: Homestead Console Table
plans.dimensions: 4ft long, table height, 12in deep
plans.extra: Part of Ana White's Homestead Collection (bench and end table also in this dataset); buildable for under $25 in lumber per the source
plans.original_description: A narrow homestead-style console table with angled 2x4 legs and a 2x6 top, part of the same collection as the Homestead Bench and End Table.
original_steps: Cut the two 2x6 top boards to 48in and the bottom shelf board to 43in.
Cut the four leg pieces from 2x4 to 27-1/8in with a 5-degree bevel on both ends, and the three sets of top cleats per the cut list.
Build the leg assemblies using pocket holes to hide fasteners.
Attach the top cleats to the underside of the tabletop, then attach the leg assemblies to the cleats.
Clamp and screw the bottom shelf in place from underneath, leaving no visible fasteners on top.
Sand with medium grit and finish with wood stain.

plans.title: Wall-Mounted Drop-Leaf Folding Table
plans.dimensions: Folded: 44in wide x 22-5/8in high, extends out 3-3/4in. Unfolded: same width/height, extends out 18-11/16in
tools: hand tools, power tools and machines (hobby level)
construction_notes: Joinery: half-lap, dowel-reinforced butt joints, and an angled notched joint reinforced with dowels
No numeric cut list in free text - full parts list with dimensions is in the source's downloadable drawing set (images), not plain text
Craftsmanspace publishes many plans as historical/public-domain-leaning free downloads - worth checking their license terms directly if reusing more from this source
plans.original_description: A wall-mounted folding table that collapses flat against the wall when not in use, using lap, dowel-reinforced butt, and notched joints rather than hinge hardware alone.
original_steps: Build the wall-mounted back bracket/frame using half-lap joints.
Build the folding leg/support assembly using dowel-reinforced butt joints and an angled notched joint that lets it fold flat.
Mount the back frame to the wall with anchors and screws.
Attach the folding top and leg assembly to the wall bracket so it swings up to a level, supported position and folds flat against the wall when not needed.

plans.title: Ultimate Craft Table with Storage Towers
plans.dimensions: 30in x 48in top, counter height (36in)
plans.extra: Uses Ana White's free online design tool to lay out the three custom storage towers; matching fence-picket pull-out crates built separately
construction_notes: No single numeric cut list published free - tower dimensions are derived per-project from the purchased top size using the source's design tool
plans.original_description: A counter-height multi-purpose craft table built around a purchased 30in x 48in butcher-block top, with three storage towers sized around a sewing machine, a cutting machine, and pull-out crates.
original_steps: Buy or source a 30in x 48in butcher block top.
Design three support towers sized to the top's dimensions - one for a sewing machine, one for a cutting machine, and one for pull-out crates.
Cut all tower pieces from 1x8 and 1x12 stock per your layout, pre-finishing the boards before assembly.
Assemble the towers with pocket holes, adding bay supports across the top of each for mounting drawer slides.
Set the top on the towers to check fit, then sand and apply several coats of polyurethane.
Install drawer slides on the bay supports, build and install the drawers, then paint the drawer faces and add pulls.
Build pull-out crates from fence pickets to slide into the open shelving.

plans.title: Ombre Mod Wall Clock
plans.dimensions: Not itemized numerically - angled board shape shown only in the source's diagram
materials: wood stain (three shades for ombre effect)
purchased round clock face with numbers
construction_notes: No numeric board dimensions in the source text - shape and angles are shown only in an image/diagram
plans.original_description: A mod-style wall clock with an ombre-stained wood plaque behind a purchased round clock face, cut with angled boards.
original_steps: Cut boards to the desired angled shape (a miter or table saw both work).
Sand all boards smooth.
Stain each board a different shade to create an ombre gradient effect.
Glue the boards together in ombre order, centering by eye, and weight with something heavy while the glue sets (about 30 minutes).
Center a purchased round clock face on the assembled plaque and mark the screw location using the clock's back notch.
Screw the clock face on snugly (flush with the back) so it can still be removed for battery changes.
Attach a sawtooth hanger to the back.

plans.title: Circle Cutting Jig for a Jigsaw
plans.dimensions: Adjustable up to a 32in circle
plans.original_description: A shop-made circle-cutting jig for a jigsaw (rather than a circular saw), using a threaded rod and hex nuts to dial in the radius, adjustable up to a 32in circle.
original_steps: Trace your jigsaw's base onto a board and cut a housing that fits it snugly.
Cut two 1x2 boards to the length of the base and glue/nail them together to form the anchor side.
Drill a hole at the front of the baseboard sized to clear the jigsaw blade.
Mount galvanized angle brackets to hold the threaded rod, which the hex nuts thread onto to set the radius.
Attach the jigsaw housing to the baseboard so the blade lines up with the pivot point.
Set the radius by threading the nuts to the desired distance, pin the pivot at the center of your workpiece, and cut the circle.

plans.title: DIY Frame for a Flat-Screen TV
plans.dimensions: Depends on the specific TV size and mounted position - not itemized as a numeric cut list
construction_notes: Exact dimensions depend on the TV size and are not itemized as a numeric cut list in the source text
plans.original_description: A DIY picture-frame-style surround built around a flat-screen or 'frame' TV from mitered trim boards, to mimic an expensive bezel at a fraction of the cost.
original_steps: Measure your TV's mounted position and determine the frame's outer dimensions, allowing clearance around the screen.
Cut and miter the trim boards to size for the four sides of the frame.
Glue and fasten the mitered corners together.
Fill nail holes and seams with caulk or filler, then sand smooth.
Paint the frame to match your decor.
Mount or position the frame around the TV.

plans.title: Multi Guitar Stand
plans.dimensions: Adjustable size to suit the number of guitars
plans.original_description: An adjustable multi-guitar stand built from 1x lumber, sized to hold several guitars side by side.
original_steps: Cut the two vertical leg supports (A) from 1x6 to 28-3/4in and sand the edges.
Cut the base components (B, C) from 1x4 and 1x3, rounding exposed corners with a jigsaw.
Drill pocket holes in the ends of the 1x3 base pieces (C) and assemble the base with glue and screws.
Center and attach the vertical leg supports (A) to the base with glue and countersunk screws.
Build and attach the neck support from 1x4, marking and cutting it to shape.
Sand all edges smooth and finish as desired.

plans.title: Vintage Camper Napkin Holder
plans.dimensions: No numeric cut list - shape comes from the source's free printable pattern
construction_notes: No numeric cut list - shape comes from the source's free printable pattern
plans.original_description: A whimsical vintage-camper-shaped napkin holder cut from scrap wood using a free printable pattern, with a dowel 'tongue jack' and drilled wheel details.
original_steps: Print the source's free camper-shaped pattern and transfer it to scrap wood.
Cut the curved camper body shape with a jigsaw or scroll saw.
Cut the napkin holder's bottom to length on a miter saw.
Drill a pilot hole and glue in a dowel 'tongue jack' to hold the front of the camper level.
Use a drill press and hole saw to cut circular 'tire' details.
Assemble the sides and tires with a brad nailer and glue.
Paint in a retro color scheme to complete the vintage-camper look.

plans.title: One-Board Wooden Centerpiece Tray
plans.dimensions: Long and narrow, for rectangular or oval tables - built from a single 1x12 @ 10ft board
construction_notes: No numeric cut list captured - board dimensions were shown only in an image/diagram in the source
plans.original_description: A long narrow centerpiece tray for a rectangular or oval dining table, built entirely from one board with routed chamfered edges and cabinet-pull handles.
original_steps: Cut a single 1x12 board down into the tray's side and bottom pieces.
Optional: rout a chamfered edge on the top edges for a more finished look.
Glue and clamp the pieces together, allowing about an hour to dry.
Sand the entire assembly smooth, including the bottom.
Stain or paint as desired.
Attach two cabinet pulls to the ends for handles.

plans.title: Portable 2x4 and Pegboard Tool Rack
plans.dimensions: Pegboard panels 23-1/2in x 4ft
plans.original_description: A portable, caster-mounted tool rack built from 2x4s and pegboard, sized to hold hand tools, craft supplies, or garden tools and move easily between rooms or job sites.
original_steps: Cut the four 6ft 2x4 legs and two 4-3/4in cross braces per the cut list, angling the ends 5 degrees.
Drill pocket holes in one end of each 6ft leg and assemble two A-frame sides using the 18in and 16-3/4in cross pieces at the base.
Attach the 4-3/4in braces near the top of each frame with glue and screws.
Attach pegboard panels to the front of each side using pegboard hooks/hardware.
Cut scrap plywood shelves to fit and attach as needed.
Mount two fixed and two locking swivel casters to the base for mobility.

plans.title: Scrap Wood Wall-Mounted Clamp Rack
plans.dimensions: Scrap-wood dependent - main board at least 18in to span two wall studs
materials: scrap wood (1 horizontal board, 2 vertical side supports, 1 top strip)
construction_notes: Dimensions are scrap-wood dependent - no fixed cut list in the source
plans.original_description: A minimal scrap-wood clamp rack that mounts to wall studs and keeps bar/pipe clamps off the floor and within reach.
original_steps: Select one horizontal scrap board at least 18in long, long enough to span two wall studs.
Cut two short vertical side supports, sized to the depth of your deepest clamp, to hold the rack off the wall.
Cut one narrow top strip for the clamps to hang from.
Attach the vertical supports to the horizontal board, then attach the top strip.
Mount the assembled rack to two wall studs for strength.
Hang clamps by their jaws over the top strip.

plans.title: Adjustable Router Table Fence
plans.dimensions: Fence base 5in x 36in x 3/4in
plans.extra: Designed as a companion to the mobile router table plan already in this dataset
plans.original_description: An adjustable router table fence with slotted support walls and t-track, so the fence face gap can adjust to different bit widths.
original_steps: Cut the 5in x 36in fence base from 3/4in plywood and drill mounting holes sized to your t-track bolts.
Cut two 15in x 5in slotted support walls and rout a 3/8in adjustment channel in each.
Assemble the support walls to the base with carriage bolts, washers, and wing nuts so the face plates can slide.
Install the t-track sections, including a horizontal track above the face plates for bit guards, stops, and featherboards.
Attach adjustable face plates to the support walls with knobs and t-track bolts.

plans.title: Jacob's Ladder Folding Block Toy
plans.dimensions: Blocks cut from a 2-1/2in wide board to 3in each
plans.original_description: A classic Jacob's Ladder folding-block toy, built from a stack of painted wood blocks connected by ribbons in a pattern that makes them appear to cascade when tipped.
original_steps: Cut a 2-1/2in wide hobby board into 3in blocks using a miter saw with a stop block for consistent length.
Sand the corners of each block round.
Paint each block a different color on each face and edge.
Cut the grosgrain ribbon into pieces sized to connect the blocks in the classic zigzag Jacob's Ladder pattern.
Attach the ribbons to the backs of the blocks with small brad nails, alternating the attachment pattern so the blocks fold and cascade.

plans.title: Toy Sink With Running Water
plans.dimensions: Sized to match the chosen plastic container/basin - no fixed cut list
construction_notes: No fixed cut list - dimensions depend entirely on the container chosen
plans.original_description: A working toy kitchen sink with a hand-pump-driven water faucet, built from wood around a plastic basin.
original_steps: Choose a plastic container to serve as the sink basin and determine the overall size from it.
Cut the wood top, front, back, and side pieces to match the container's footprint.
Join the pieces with pocket holes, building the top first, then the front and back, then the sides.
Sand all surfaces smooth.
Build a faucet from a pipe flange, PVC pipe, and elbow fittings, then spray paint it to match the knobs.
Drill and install spinning knobs using a machine screw, nuts, and an end cap.
Install a submersible pump with tubing routed up through the faucet.

plans.title: One-Board Bathroom Floating Shelves
plans.dimensions: One 1x6 @ 6ft board yields two shelves
construction_notes: Exact shelf dimensions were not given as a numeric cut list in the source text
plans.original_description: A pair of simple floating shelves cut from a single 6ft board, built without pocket holes for a fast beginner-friendly build.
original_steps: Select a 1x6 board about 6ft long - one board yields two shelves.
Cut the board into the shelf top, bottom, and side pieces for two shelves.
Glue and screw the pieces together, reinforcing with a nail gun.
Sand and stain each shelf.
Mount to the wall using your preferred floating-shelf hardware.

plans.title: Dachshund-Shaped CD Holder
plans.dimensions: Pattern-based (1in grid printout)
plans.original_description: A dachshund-shaped CD holder cut from a printable pattern, using bent metal flashing brackets as supports for the CD stack.
original_steps: Print the body pattern in landscape mode at 100% (grid squares should be 1in) and transfer it to 3/4in pine.
Cut two each of the front and back leg shapes, and rip a short piece of 2in wide stock in half for the 3/8in-thick ears.
Cut two body/back pieces and sand all pieces before gluing the parts together.
Cut two 3in lengths of metal flashing for support brackets, filing the edges and rounding the corners.
Drill mounting holes in the brackets and dimple the back pieces to match, then fasten with wood screws.

plans.title: Two-Tone Salt and Pepper Shakers
plans.dimensions: Not published in the free text - source points to a separate downloadable plan for exact measurements
materials: maple (accent wood, salvaged pallet wood usable)
construction_notes: This source doesn't publish its own numeric dimensions in the free text - it points to a separate downloadable plan
plans.original_description: A second approach to wooden salt and pepper shakers, using contrasting woods (sapele body, maple accent) resawn on the bandsaw - a good beginner batch-gift project.
original_steps: Joint two faces each of your primary (sapele) and accent (maple) stock.
Resaw thin accent strips from the maple on the bandsaw.
Laminate the primary and accent strips together with glue.
Turn or shape the shaker bodies to your chosen profile (a lathe works well for round shakers).
Drill the seasoning cavity, dispensing holes, and stopper hole.
Finish with a food-safe finish.

plans.title: Modern Leaf-Shaped Plant Stand / Magazine Rack
plans.dimensions: Legs about 32in, short braces about 18in, long brace about 32in
plans.original_description: A modern leaf/panel-shaped magazine rack and plant stand combo cut from a single sheet of oak plywood, standing on angled dowel legs.
original_steps: Cut the plywood panel to your chosen leaf or panel shape using a template.
Drill angled holes with a Forstner bit for the dowel legs, using an angled drill attachment for a splayed stance.
Cut the dowel legs (about 32in) and short/long braces (about 18in and 32in) to length.
Assemble the dowel legs into the panel using glue and pocket screws for the cross braces.
Sand and finish as desired.

plans.title: Shiplap Mailbox Stand
plans.dimensions: Standard mailbox height 42in above ground (about 45in total with curb allowance)
plans.original_description: A modern shiplap-look mailbox stand built as a simple 1x4 frame around a mailbox post, set in concrete, with shiplap trim added for texture.
original_steps: Remove the old mailbox post and dig new post holes.
Set the new post in the hole and add quick-dry concrete to stabilize it.
Build a basic frame from 1x4s cut to the post height (standard is 42in above ground, about 45in total accounting for a curb).
Attach the frame around the post.
Add shiplap-style trim boards over the frame for the finished look.
Mount the mailbox and add house numbers to the side.

plans.title: Sectioned Scrap Wood Storage Cart
plans.dimensions: Shop-specific - sized to the builder's own scrap inventory (source example: panels up to 38in wide)
construction_notes: No fixed cut list - the source designs this per-shop based on the builder's own scrap pile dimensions
plans.original_description: A mobile, multi-section plywood cart for sorting and storing plywood offcuts and scrap boards by size, built to roll out of the way when not needed.
original_steps: Decide on your section sizes based on your own scrap inventory (e.g. large panels in back, thin strips on one side, mid-length pieces up front).
Build the cart's outer box from plywood.
Cut and install internal dividers to create the sections.
Attach casters to the base for mobility.
Sort existing scrap into the new sections by size.

plans.title: Garden Arbor Bench with Trellis Sides
plans.dimensions: 6x6 posts buried 2ft deep in concrete
cut_list: 4 - 6x6 @ 10ft (posts, notched at top, buried 2ft in concrete)
plans.extra: Source site also offers a free PDF download
plans.original_description: A garden arbor with a built-in bench and trellis sides, with 6x6 posts buried 2ft in concrete and a 2x6 seat/backrest.
original_steps: Cut and notch the four 6x6 posts at the top per the plan, then set them 2ft deep in concrete.
Cut two 2x6 boards to 7ft 11in, notch the ends, and install near the top of the posts.
Cut and install five 2x2s at 3ft 11in below that.
Cut two 2x4s to 4ft 11in, notch the posts, and install.
Cut five 2x4s to 2ft and assemble the seat support frame.
Cut seven 2x6 boards to 4ft for the seat and backrest and install with deck screws.
Build the side trellises from 2x2s and attach to the sides.

plans.title: Pine and Plain Open Shelf Console Table
